channel.title: Carat Capital — The Trade Paper of the Jewelry World
channel.description: Prices, intelligence and analysis from every desk of the stone trade: diamonds, gold and metals, gemstones, watches, auctions, retail and technology.

item.title: The $144 day: gold breaks its quiet week early
item.description: Kitco's late-Tuesday board read $4,131, up 1.33%, after a stretch that ran from $3,998.80 at the low to $4,142.70 at the high. Silver traded to $60.11, platinum added 2.28%, and the Fed is still six days out.

item.title: Seventy percent old gold: India's counter learns to recycle
item.description: The World Gold Council's July update shows imports down 42% in June to $1.97 billion, the lowest since June 2025, while some retailers report old-gold exchanges at up to 70% of jewelry sales and April-June revenue up 30-60%.

item.title: Gold up fifty, diamonds down fifty-eight: Luk Fook's split quarter
item.description: Retail sales rose 32% in April-June and gold same-store sales gained 50%, but diamond same-store sales fell 17% group-wide and 58% on the mainland. Chow Sang Sang grew 16%. Both chains kept closing stores.

item.title: ‘Disposable tools’: the miners’ union answers De Beers
item.description: The National Union of Mineworkers has condemned the two-year Venetia pause that puts 1,134 mine employees and 80 sales staff into consultation, accusing De Beers of presenting a predetermined decision and proposing cuts that start in the executive suite.

item.title: Sixty-one percent: the aisle has already voted
item.description: The Knot's 2026 Real Weddings study finds 61% of couples who married in 2025 chose lab-grown center stones. Wholesale prices fell 14-15% in the first quarter; a premium one-carat lab stone now retails for $800-$1,200.

item.title: Thirty-five percent at midnight: Brazil's rough rides free
item.description: A 25% Section 301 tariff on Brazilian goods takes effect July 23, stacking to 35% on polished stones and finished jewelry. Rough gemstones and unwrought precious metals are exempt, and the math may soon read 37.5%.

item.title: Graff pins the butterfly: twelve brooches built to tremble
item.description: A new high-jewelry capsule revives the 17th-century jabot pin with en tremblant wings, Paraíba tourmalines, invisibly set pink sapphires and a house motif dating to 1975. Every piece converts to a pendant. Prices on request.

item.title: Piaget doubles at Bonhams, and the trophy era loosens
item.description: Record spring sales saw Piaget models double and triple in value, a 1970s Cartier Bamboo Coussin bring $114,800 and a gold Polo Jumbo $33,280 — while a 1930s Rolex Prince went for $2,176. The market is widening, not just rising.

item.title: ‘The rumours were true’: Bennahmias tried to buy De Bethune
item.description: The former Audemars Piguet chief confirms a failed run at the 500-piece independent now owned by The 1916 Company, just as DB25 Starry Varius results run $42,000 to $135,500 at auction, consistently over estimate.

item.title: Plus 11.2%: June hands Swiss watchmaking its month back
item.description: Exports reached CHF 2.4 billion in June on 1.3 million pieces — the strongest month of the year. America added 12.7%, France more than doubled, and the CHF 200–500 segment grew 54.1%. The half still reads −0.7%.

item.title: £133 million, up 76%: Watches of Switzerland answers its doubters
item.description: Statutory pre-tax profit jumped 76% to £133 million ($170 million) for the year — six weeks after the board waved off interest at a reported £1.75 billion. IDEX reports the result has buyers circling again.

item.title: 33,000 carats after the end: Diavik sweeps its own floor
item.description: Rio Tinto's Canadian mine closed in March. The second quarter still yielded 33,000 carats from remaining ore — down 97% — and first-half output fell 51% to 1.1 million carats. Sales run through year-end. Then a 154-year-old miner is out of diamonds.

item.title: 1,214 jobs on the table: the union answers Venetia's pause
item.description: South Africa's National Union of Mineworkers says De Beers' two-year halt puts 1,134 mine workers and 80 sales staff into Section 189A retrenchment consultation — and demands executive costs share the pain.

item.title: Gold idles at $4,008: one quiet week left before the Fed speaks
item.description: Kitco's Monday board closed at $4,000.30, down 0.42%; Tuesday's early tape reads $4,008, near flat. Silver held $56.36 after leading Monday at +1.22%. A firm dollar and 10-year yields in the mid-4s keep the lid on — until July 28–29.

item.title: Up 8.6%, in fewer boxes: America's half-year at the jewelry counter
item.description: US jewelry revenue grew 8.6% in the first half and 13% in June, per Tenoris — but the average ticket rose 19% while unit sales fell. Bracelets led at +14%; lab-grown grew on units, natural diamonds on price.

item.title: Same-store, plus 39%: Hong Kong's gold counters run hot
item.description: Luk Fook's June quarter: retail value +32%, gold product sales +50% same-store — while diamond sales fell 17%, and 58% on the mainland. Chow Sang Sang adds +16%. A mainland VAT change is pushing buyers across the border.

item.title: Four times the estimate: the pearl keeps proving the trade wrong
item.description: Tenoris counts pearl jewelry among the surprises of America's first half; at Bonhams London this month, a two-row natural strand made £39,680 against a £10,000 estimate. Retail data and the auction floor are telling the same story.

item.title: Four thousand, defended: gold walks into Fed week on its floor
item.description: Gold closed Friday up $42.10 at $4,017.30 after touching $3,959, and opened Monday at $4,018. December hike odds sit near 82%, Brent crude at $90.40 — and the July 28–29 Fed meeting decides whether the floor is real.

item.title: Up 26.5% in a month: India's export machine restarts
item.description: Gems and jewellery exports rose 26.5% in June to $2.21 billion, per GJEPC data. Gold jewellery did the heavy lifting at +54.5%; cut-and-polished diamonds added 8.7% — the category's first clean monthly rise in the slump's long tail.

item.title: Botswana answers Penny: partner, pre-empt, or bring a friend
item.description: Minister Moeti Mohwasa told parliament the state's 15% of De Beers carries 'complete freedom' — join the Penny consortium, exercise pre-emption alone, or bid with a third party. Two bidders remain from six; close is eyed by Q4.

item.title: Carbon-neutral, sanction-locked: Alrosa's green crown has no throne
item.description: TÜV Austria has certified all 29.8 million carats of Alrosa's 2025 output carbon-neutral, a second straight year. The footprint improved 60% to −1.18 kg CO2e a carat. G7 sanctions still bar the stones from Western counters.

item.title: Fifteen tonnes of steel, and a conscience: Oris does the math
item.description: Oris mills about 15 tonnes of steel a year — ten cars' worth — so CEO Rolf Studer counts the real footprint in travel and shipping. New packaging cut emissions 50%; the causes run from New York oysters to Yangtze porpoises.

item.title: Berkshire's jeweler splits the job: revenue here, factories there
item.description: Richline promotes Rio Grande president Arien Gessner to chief revenue officer across four divisions and hands 30-year veteran Moss Makhoulian supply chain — procurement, Dominican and Chinese plants, logistics and diamond supply.

item.title: Only 30% haven't: the counter quietly adopts AI
item.description: ONS survey data says just 30% of UK retailers haven't touched AI. Of the users, 46% put it on operations, 25.4% on personalisation — and 10.8% use it to replace roles. The trade's handshake culture meets the model.

item.title: Sixty-six dollars a carat: Gemfields invents the ruby middle market
item.description: Gemfields' first 'Trade Select' auction cleared 348,409 carats — 93.2% of the offer — for $23.1 million at a $66.30 average, with sapphire categories debuting alongside. Mid-grade African color now has price discovery of its own.

item.title: Down 3.4% in a war week: gold's oldest rule just broke
item.description: Spot gold closed Friday at $3,985.80 — its worst week in six — while airstrikes on Iran entered a sixth day. December rate-hike odds hit 73%, and the market decided a war can be a reason to sell bullion.

item.title: £105 million in, £7.8 million out: Audemars Piguet's British paradox
item.description: AP's UK sales jumped 29% in 2025 — nearly double their 2022 level — while operating profit slipped 3% to £7.8 million. The new Manchester AP House alone sold £7.4 million and still lost £738,000.

item.title: Serial 022 leaves the safe: the first Résonance ever sold heads to Geneva
item.description: One owner, 27 years, a serial number chosen to match his birth date. Phillips will offer F.P. Journe Chronomètre à Résonance no. 022/99R — almost certainly the first commercially delivered — on November 7–8, estimated at CHF 500,000 to 1 million.

item.title: Rolex, rejected: 38% fail one marketplace's inspection
item.description: Bezel turns away 38% of the Rolex watches submitted to its authenticators — against 13% for Omega, 6% for Tudor and 5% for Cartier. Rejected Rolexes account for 43% of all value refused; Patek Philippe another 22%.

item.title: Seventy dollars to Southampton: the jewelry trade pitches its summer tent
item.description: The Hamptons Jewelry Show returns July 23–26 — 36,000 square feet at the Southampton Fairgrounds, a $200 opening preview, $70 day passes, and two new pavilions aimed squarely at where American luxury money spends August.
